do, and handles transformations that neither of those can: computing totals, building lookup objects, grouping items by category, and collapsing arrays into any shape of data in a single pass.

processes every element of an array and combines them into a single output value. That output can be a number, string, object, array, boolean — anything. The method takes a callback function and an initial value. It calls the callback once per element, passing in an
(the running result so far) and the current element. Whatever the callback returns becomes the new accumulator for the next iteration. After the last element, the final accumulator value is returned.
The element being processed in this iteration
Zero-based index of the current element (optional)
The starting value of the accumulator — always provide this

Building a Lookup Object (Indexing by ID)
One of the most practical uses in React is turning an array into an object keyed by ID, enabling O(1) lookups instead of repeatedly scanning the array:
In React: Aggregating State & Derived Data
Many React apps normalize API data into an
map on load, so any component can look up any item by ID without searching. This is the pattern Redux Toolkit's
hook is conceptually the same pattern as
: it takes a current state (accumulator), an action (current element), and returns the next state. Understanding
When you need a plain transformation (same shape, different values),
is cleaner. When you need to exclude items,
when the output has a fundamentally different shape from the input.

uses the first array element as the starting accumulator and skips it in the loop. On an empty array this throws. When the accumulator is an object or array (not a number), omitting the initial value causes the first element — an object or array — to be used as-is as the accumulator, leading to hard-to-debug mutations and incorrect shapes.
Mutating the accumulator instead of returning a new one
Using reduce() when map() or filter() is simpler
It prevents crashes on empty arrays, makes the accumulator type explicit, and makes the code readable — the reader immediately knows what shape the result will be.
Choose the right method for the job.
when the output shape differs from the input (sum, object, grouped structure). Use
for same-shape transformations and selections — they communicate intent more clearly.
when the shape is meaningful — it communicates what is being built.
